State-of-the-art image restoration networks show promising results, but are largely limited to well-illuminated scenes.
Image restoration networks' performance drops significantly when photon shot noise is strong.
In this paper, a new blur estimation technique customized for photon-limited conditions is proposed.
The proposed method employs a gradient-based backpropagation method to estimate the blur kernel.
The blur kernel is modeled using a low-dimensional representation with key points on the motion trajectory.
Modeling the blur kernel with a low-dimensional representation significantly reduces the search space for kernel estimation.
Modeling the blur kernel with a low-dimensional representation improves the regularity of kernel estimation.
When plugged into an iterative framework, the novel low-dimensional representation provides improved kernel estimates.
Improved kernel estimates lead to significantly better deconvolution performance compared to end-to-end trained neural networks.

The terms F and T are two neural network computations used in the context. The gradient ∇zL(zk) is computed using auto-differentiation tools provided in PyTorch by backpropagating the gradients through F and then T.

In the second stage, the kernel estimate from Stage I is fine-tuned by 'opening up' the search space to the entire kernel vector instead of parametrizing by T. This involves optimizing the loss function L(h) which includes an ℓ1-norm sparsity prior.

The optimization problem involves the kernel vector h being optimized instead of the latent key point vector z. The iterative updates involve updating hk+1 and vk+1 based on certain conditions and constraints.
The input for the algorithm is described as Noisy-blurry y and Photon-Level α. The denoiser G(·), non-blind solver F(·), and Kernel-Trajectory-Network T(·) are also mentioned in the algorithm.

The term L(h) represents the loss function while ∇hL(hk) represents the gradient of the loss function with respect to h. The gradient of the loss function is calculated using automatic differentiation

The term hk represents the current value of h, while h(∞) represents the final converged value of h after the iterative process
The value of hk is updated using a combination of gradient descent term δ∇hL(hk) and a term proportional to the difference between current hk and previous computed value vk
